coredata.dc:title: Tuning magnetic and luminescent properties of iron oxide@C nanoparticles from hydrothermal synthesis: Influence of precursor reagents
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva, Atailson Oliveira
Campos, Alex Fabiano Cortez
Rodrigues, Marcelo Oliveira
Sousa, Marcelo Henrique
coredata.dc:description: Magneto-luminescent nanocomposites were obtained by coating coprecipitated iron oxide nanoparticles with a functionalized carbon layer by hydrothermal treatment, using different organic precursors (D-glucose and p-phenylenediamine). The nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), RAMAN, infrared (FTIR), UV-vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic analysis, vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) and zetametry. Remarkable contrasting changes in the structural and magnetic properties of nanocomposites were identified after the carbon coating, which was directly related to the precursor used. Crystalline phase transformations were observed with a decrease in the saturation magnetization (-80%) using p-phenylenediamine and an increase (+22%) using glucose, relative to uncoated magnetic core. The distinct photoluminescent properties with multicolored emissions on long-wavelength are ascribed to the carbon shell and to the chemical moieties, such as amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups on the surface. This makes it possible for different surface states to be responsible for emission, and these chemical groups are also responsible for a higher colloidal stability of nanocomposites in alkaline medium. The tunability of the properties of nanocomposites from the conditions of synthesis opens a wide range of applications for these nanomaterials, such as ion detection, magnetohyperthermia, and theranostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Luminescent
Magnetic
Carbon coating
Multicolored emission
Magnetization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 2.1 Synthesis of the magnetic nanoparticle cores (MNPs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the following Brazilian Agencies: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES) and Fundação de Apoio à Pesquisa do Distrito Federal (FAPDF). M.H.S. acknowledges CNPq grant number 305481/2018-5 and FAPDF grant number 193-00000188/2019-58. The authors would like to thank Dr. Tatiane Oliveira dos Santos from the High-Resolution Microscopy Multi-User Laboratory at the Federal University of Goiás, Brazil, for the electron microscopy measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Scheme of the elaboration of magnetic nanocomposites, and (b) photo of diluted sol samples.
TEM images (left); magnified HRTEM images of the nanoparticles (right) – the inset shows the calculated FFTs of the square areas for (a) MNP, (b) MNP@C-G and (c) MNP@C-P.
X-ray diffractograms of samples. Maghemite peaks are labeled as “m” and hematite peaks as “h”. The inset shows the (511) 2θ peak region for samples MNP and MNP@C-G.
Magnetization curves of samples. The inset shows the recorded hysteresis cycles in the low field range.
(a) FTIR and (b) Raman spectra of the prepared samples.
Zeta potential as a function of pH for the studied samples.
(a) Absorbance and Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of MNP@C-G and (b) MNP@C-P.

head.ce:title: Tuning magnetic and luminescent properties of iron oxide@C nanoparticles from hydrothermal synthesis: Influence of precursor reagents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Inorganics and Materials - LIMA, Institute of Chemistry, University of Brasília, DF, Brasília 70904-970, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Inorganics and Materials - LIMA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory for Environmental and Applied Nanoscience, FUP, University of Brasília, DF, Planaltina 73345-010, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory for Environmental and Applied Nanoscience, FUP

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Nanotechnology Group, University of Brasília, DF, Ceilândia 72220-275, Brazil
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cGreen Nanotechnology Group, University of Brasília, DF, Ceilândia 72220-275, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magneto-luminescent nanocomposites were obtained by coating coprecipitated iron oxide nanoparticles with a functionalized carbon layer by hydrothermal treatment, using different organic precursors (D-glucose and p-phenylenediamine). The nanomaterials were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), RAMAN, infrared (FTIR), UV-vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopic analysis, vibrating sample magnetometry (VSM) and zetametry. Remarkable contrasting changes in the structural and magnetic properties of nanocomposites were identified after the carbon coating, which was directly related to the precursor used. Crystalline phase transformations were observed with a decrease in the saturation magnetization (-80%) using p-phenylenediamine and an increase (+22%) using glucose, relative to uncoated magnetic core. The distinct photoluminescent properties with multicolored emissions on long-wavelength are ascribed to the carbon shell and to the chemical moieties, such as amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups on the surface. This makes it possible for different surface states to be responsible for emission, and these chemical groups are also responsible for a higher colloidal stability of nanocomposites in alkaline medium. The tunability of the properties of nanocomposites from the conditions of synthesis opens a wide range of applications for these nanomaterials, such as ion detection, magnetohyperthermia, and theranostics.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The advances in the techniques of preparation and characterization of nanostructured materials have made the search for multifunctional materials more and more frequent [1]. Depending on their synthesis method, the composition and morphology can be tuned to produce multifunctional materials with unique physical and chemical properties. Particularly, the nanocomposites – multiphase materials in which at least one phase has a nanoscale structure [2] – can exhibit enhanced surface, optical and magnetic properties, and higher biocompatibility when compared to conventional nanomaterials such as oxides, metals, carbon and semiconductor nanoparticles.

In the case of carbon-based nanocomposites, the coating of metallic-based nanoparticles with carbon has been widely used to ensure thermostability, increased oxidation and acid erosion resistance [3], biocompatibility, chemical stability in several environments, and improved magnetic properties [4]. Many functional groups on the carbon surface may also be easily modified, making these types of nanocomposites highly suitable for adsorption [5], functionalization [6], and energy storage in lithium batteries [7].

Despite the existence of well-established methods for the elaboration of magneto-luminescent structures using metallic oxides (such as iron oxides), there are inherent limitations regarding the toxicity and biocompatibility of these materials [8,9]. An alternative strategy by which to avoid this constraint is the formation of carbon layers in these nanoparticles, which improve their biocompatibility and capacity for intermediate interactions with ions, molecules, and organisms in solution [10] As a beneficial consequence, the carbon-coating of magnetic nanoparticles has been used to detect pathogenic bacteria [11–13], promote the transport of medicines [6], and remove toxic metals such as lead [14,15] and uranium [16] from contaminated water. However, the process of capping these iron oxide cores with carbon layers can bring changes in the structural, magnetic and optical properties of the core and nanocomposites, an issue that has been little explored in the literature. Even so, many of these properties can have appealing uses, such as medical applications taking advantage of the potential of the nanocomposites as luminescent markers. They may also have environmental purposes, exploring the variations in their luminescent spectra in the presence of adsorbed ions or molecules (biosensors).

The precursors used to generate the carbon layer on the surface of the magnetic nanoparticles play a fundamental role in the final properties of the nanocomposite. Indeed, depending on the type of the molecule and synthesis route used, the formed layer can promote an increase [10] or decrease [17] in the magnetization of nanocomposites. Moreover, after this surface-modification step, optical properties can arise as excitation-dependent multicolor emissions, a characteristic that is commonly observed in carbon dots [18]. Both optical and magnetic properties open a wide range of possibilities in the application of nanocomposites in different fields of study.

In this survey, we propose the elaboration and characterization of magneto-luminescent nanocomposites, obtained by coprecipitation of an iron oxide-based core followed by its carbon-coating using a hydrothermal route, with a core@shell architecture: a magnetic core surrounded by a shell with luminescent properties (MNP@C). Two different precursors (D-glucose and p-phenylenediamine) were used to form the carbon layers, since these substances are widely employed to obtain carbon dots with excitation-dependent emission in the visible spectrum [19,20]. However, the use of these precursors in elaborating magneto-luminescent nanocomposites and their impact on structural, morphological, and magnetic properties has still been little studied in the literature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to evaluate the structural and morphological characteristics of the obtained nanocomposites and confirm their core@shell design. The identification of the chemical groups and bonds was carried out by using Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) and RAMAN spectroscopy, while the optical properties were assessed by ultraviolet-visible absorption and photoluminescence measurements. Also, the magnetic properties of the nanoengineered materials were investigated by Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM) and, lastly, the colloidal stability was evaluated at different pH conditions by zeta potential experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2.1 Synthesis of the magnetic nanoparticle cores (MNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: As schematized in Fig. 1a , maghemite nanoparticles were synthesized using a modified procedure described in the literature [21] and used as the magnetic core for further carbon coating. Briefly, 500 mL of NH4OH (2 mol·L−1) was dripped, at a constant rate of 6 mL per minute, into a mixture containing 150 ml of 1 mol·L−1 of FeCl3·6H2O and 75 ml of 1 mol·L−1 FeSO4·7H2O, under vigorous magnetic stirring (900 rpm) at room temperature and maintained for 30 min, after the total addition of the basic solution. The formed precipitate (magnetite – Fe3O4) was magnetically separated and washed with water several times until the pH reached neutrality. Then, the medium was acidified with 100 mL of HNO3 (1 mol·L−1) under stirring for 25 min. The Fe3O4 precipitate was oxidized to obtain the maghemite nanoparticles (γ-Fe2O3) by adding 100 mL of Fe(NO3)3·7H2O (0.5 mol·L−1) and heating the system to the boiling temperature for 30 min. After cooling, the nanoparticles were washed with acetone three times, and after total evaporation of acetone, they were redispersed in water to obtain a magnetic colloidal dispersion. Solid MNPs were obtained by drying part of the magnetic colloidal dispersion in an oven at 80°C for further surface coating with carbon, as described in the next section.

As schematized in Fig. 1a, 0.3 g of MNPs were dispersed in 30 mL of an aqueous solution containing 4 mmol of ammonium acetate (CH3COONH4) and sonicated for 20 min. Then, 20 mL of an aqueous solution containing 2 mmol of D-glucose (C6H12O6) was added to the system. The resulting material was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated to 160°C for 10h. The obtained product was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min, washed three times with water, then with acetone, and then dried in a desiccator for 48 h for further characterization. This procedure was repeated, replacing D-glucose (C6H12O6) with p-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), maintaining the proportions and synthesis parameters. The nanocomposites obtained using p-phenylenediamine and D-glucose were labeled as MNP@C-P and MNP@C-G, respectively. Fig. 1b shows the color of diluted sols obtained at each step of synthesis.

The size and morphology of the as-produced materials were examined using a 400 mesh carbon-coated copper grid in a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd. Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) powder analyses of the samples were carried out in a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer model Miniflex 600 with Cu-Ka radiation (λ=1.54 Å). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a spectrophotometer, Shimadzu model IRPrestige-21. RAMAN spectra were obtained with a confocal microscope model LAbRAM HR Evolution Horiba coupled to a CCD (Charge Coupled Device), using a green (532 nm) semiconductor diode laser with a grid of 18000 line/mm. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectra of aqueous dispersions were recorded in a Hitachi UV-vis spectrophotometer model U-3900H, and the fluorescence spectra were measured on a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer model F-7000. Room temperature magnetization curves were obtained using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) ADE model EV7. Hysteresis loops were recorded in the ± 18 kOe range. The zeta potential of the nanoparticles was determined as a function of pH. The readings were carried out in a ZetaSizer (Malvern, model NanoZS) with a disposable cuvette. The results of electrophoretic mobilities were converted into zeta potential (ζ) values using the Henry equation [22].

Fig. 2 exhibits the TEM images of the three synthesized samples. The unshelled maghemite nanoparticles in the MNP sample are nearly spherical (Fig. 2a) and present an average diameter (and polydispersity) of 9.8 ± 0.32 nm (0.33 ± 0.04), calculated from particles in the distribution histogram, fitted with the log-normal function (Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material). This mean size is typical of the nanoparticles obtained using the coprecipitation route employed in this survey [23].

After the hydrothermal step in the sample treated with D-glucose (MNP@C-G), the nearly spherical morphology remains almost unaltered (Fig. 2b). However, the average diameter (and polydispersity) of nanoparticles changed to 13.7 ± 0.27 nm (0.23 ± 0.02) – see Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material. This size increase may be primarily attributed to the deposition of an amorphous carbon layer on the MNP core, as shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 2b). Nevertheless, in the sample treated with p-phenylenediamine (MNP@C-P), larger hexagonal nanoparticles were formed, coexisting with ∼10 nm-sized nearly spherical nanoparticles (Fig. 2c). Moreover, the absence of isolated carbon nanostructures (C-dots) in TEM images of both samples, which could have been formed during the hydrothermal treatment, corroborates a successful deposition of carbon, preferentially on the surface of magnetic cores. With similar results, Zhao et al. [24] reported that ammonium acetate, when used as an additive in hydrothermal carbon coating, plays a critical role in the generation of carbon shell in Fe3O4 nanoparticles, decreasing the formation of isolated C-dot side structures and contributing to the growth of a more uniform carbon layer on the surface of magnetic nanoparticles.

The XRD patterns obtained for all samples investigated are shown in Fig. 3 . The main crystalline phase observed in sample MNP corresponds to the cubic spinel- structured of maghemite (γ-Fe2O3), with space group Fd3m (JCPDS 39-1346) determined from Rietveld refinement (Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material). The crystalline mean size of these magnetic core nanoparticles was also estimated from XRD data refinement and corresponds to 12.9 nm.

In sample MNP@C-G, the diffraction peaks of the cubic spinel phase were well preserved (Fig. 3). Nevertheless, the peaks are slightly dislocated towards the left (inset of Fig. 3), indicating the conversion of the maghemite core to magnetite [25], when using D-glucose in the carbon coating procedure. XRD peaks of magnetite and maghemite are very similar, however, the presence of equivalent peaks slightly shifted towards the lower angles indicates the predominance of the magnetite phase. Particularly, the quantitative shift of the (511) XRD peak towards 57° indicates the complete conversion of maghemite into magnetite. Thus, the XRD pattern of sample MNP@C-G was analyzed using Rietveld structure refinement with the cubic spinel-structured magnetite phase (Fe3O4), with space group Fd3m (JCPDS 19-0629). The crystalline diameter of MNP@C-G nanoparticles slightly increased (to 14.5 nm) compared to the unshelled maghemite, indicating the preservation of the initial magnetic core.

In sample MNP@C-P, however, a new group of peaks coexist with those of the spinel structure (of low intensity). These peaks coincide with the diffraction pattern of hematite (α-Fe2O3) rhombohedral structure, with space group R3c (JCPDS 33-0664), which was utilized with the maghemite phase in the Rietveld refinement for this sample (Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material). The analysis revealed a relative proportion of hematite/maghemite equals 58/42 (%). The crystalline sizes of the nanoparticles were estimated in both phases of the diffractogram as ∼92 nm, from the hematite peaks, and ∼12.5 nm from the maghemite peaks, which indicates that the larger particles in the TEM images (Fig. 2c) correspond to hematite.

Moreover, in both composites, the lack of a broad peak close to 2θ = 20° also indicates that pure C-dot nanoparticles [26] are absent and that the carbon coating of the magnetic cores in our samples presents an amorphous character [14,24].

The Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis of HRTEM images was carried out in single particles, assessing the spot diffraction pattern regions (Fig. 2). Data analysis indicates that some spots in Fig. 2a correspond to the (220), (311), (111), and (222) planes of the spinel phase in sample MNP. This cubic structure was also confirmed in the core of nanostructures of sample MNP@C-G, which presents the planes (111), (311), (220), and (222). In sample MNP@C-P (Fig. 2c), a typical rhombohedral structure of hematite with planes (104), (012), (113) and (110) is present, along with the spinel phase with planes (111), (220), (311), and (222). These FFT results agree well with XRD data, showing that the crystalline structure of the magnetic core is preserved in sample MNP@C-G, while in sample MNP@C-P there is a phase transformation in the iron oxide core structure after the hydrothermal treatment.

Some studies point out that glucose behaves as a reducing and coating agent for the formation of nanoscaled Fe3O4 from a Fe3+ precursor under hydrothermal conditions [27,28]. Indeed, Sun et al [28] have reported the formation of α-Fe2O3 instead of magnetite from a synthesis performed without glucose using the same reaction procedure. In the case of sample MNP@C-G, the glucose possibly acts as a coating agent in the hydrothermal treatment, attenuating the dissolution and leaching of Fe3+ ions to form much larger hematite (or other oxides/hydroxides) particles. Also, due to its reducing characteristic, glucose partly reduces Fe3+ ions to Fe2+ in situ into the smaller maghemite structures to form the Fe3O4 phase in sample MNP@C-G.

Li et al. [29] have shown that the formation of hematite from maghemite, under hydrothermal conditions in an oxidative environment, involves the dissolution of pre-existent maghemite nanoparticles and the reprecipitation of Fe3+ ions as α-Fe2O3 between 120 and 180°C. Due to the low solubility of maghemite, the concentration of ferric species is low during this process and the precipitation of hematite likely proceeds based on a small number of nuclei that slowly grow into large crystals. It agrees with our XRD results and with the TEM/HRTEM images (Fig. 2c), which show large crystals with morphologies very likely corresponding to hematite [30,31] in sample MNP@C-P.

Fig. 4 shows the magnetization curves (hysteresis loops) of the elaborated samples at 300 K. As shown in the low-field region, the coercive field and remanent magnetization are negligible, suggesting the occurrence of thermal relaxation states of magnetic moments (superparamagnetism) at 300 K, which is also preserved after the carbon coating.

The saturation magnetization (Ms) of sample MNP, ∼ 43 emu/g, is compatible with the value for pristine maghemite NPs of similar size [25]. The saturation magnetization of sample MNP@C-G increased by approximately 22% compared to MNP sample. Lee et al. [32] have reported analogous results in Fe3O4/carbon core@shell nanoparticles, produced by a similar route of synthesis. In that work, carbon encapsulation significantly enhanced the room-temperature magnetization of the nanostructures, primarily caused by the chemical reduction of the Fe3+ ions in the A-sites. In the present study, we also ascribe the Ms increase for sample MNP@C-G to the conversion/reduction, in the magnetic core, of γ-Fe2O3 into Fe3O4 during the hydrothermal treatment [32], whose saturation magnetization is 20-30% higher than that of maghemite [25].

A distinct magnetization result was obtained for the sample MNP@C-P. Here, the Ms value decreases about 80% after the hydrothermal treatment with p-phenylenediamine. This behavior may be related to the partial conversion of maghemite into hematite during this process since hematite presents a weakly ferromagnetic or antiferromagnetic characteristic at room temperature [33]. Thus, the residual magnetization of the MNP@C-P sample arises from the remaining maghemite phase preserved after the hydrothermal treatment.

Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectroscopies were used as a tool to probe surface modification. The obtained FTIR spectra are presented and compared in Fig. 5 a. The peaks at 570 cm−1 and 480 and 418 cm−1, seen in sample MNP and assigned to the Fe‑oxygen bonds in tetrahedral and octahedral sites in the iron oxide [34,35] are still present in the carbon-coated samples. Besides, a hydroxyl stretching vibration band, present at 3450–3150 cm−1, is more intense in the case of the nanocomposite samples. The single hydroxyl bending vibration band at 1622 cm−1, along with the two hydroxyl bending vibration bands at 898 cm−1 and 798 cm−1, are an important diagnosis of the presence of FeO(OH) moieties [36] in all samples. The occurrence of the peaks at 545 cm−1, 632 cm−1 and 686 cm−1 (forbidden for a spinel structure), overlapped in a wide band, indicates a tetragonal vacancy-ordered superlattice [37]. The intensity of this band makes the bands related to Fe-O stretching at 570 cm−1 and 470 cm−1 less evident. Some peaks of sample MNP are observed to a lesser extent or overlapping those of the nanocomposites from organic layers. The spectrum of MNP@C-G has a bandwidth ranging from 3630 cm−1 to 2800 cm−1, which is characteristic of O-H, N-H and C-H stretching vibrations. The band at 1695 cm−1 may be assigned to C=O stretching vibration. Also, the bandwidth observed in this region reinforces the presence of C=N, C-N-C and C=C. The multiple peaks in the region of 1550-1300 cm−1 may be attributed to O-C-H and C-O-H absorption, followed by C-N stretching at 1295 cm−1. Below 665 cm−1, the spectrum is quite similar to that of sample MNP.

In the spectrum of sample MNP@C-P, the more intense band at 3450-3150 cm−1 may be attributed to a greater number of O-H groups at 3433 cm−1 and the emergence of N-H stretching modes at 3200 cm−1. Also, the characteristic C=O and C-O stretching vibrations at 1683 cm− 1 and at 1327 cm−1, respectively, may be assigned to aromatic carboxylic acids. The bands at 1640 cm−1, 1384 cm−1, and 1600 cm−1 may be related to C=N, C-N-C, and C=C stretching vibrations, which indicate the formation of polyaromatic structures [35]. The bandwidth close to 717 cm−1 may be associated with N-H from primary and secondary amines and C-H from aromatic rings.

These FTIR results indicate that the nanocomposites present many functional groups on the surface, mainly amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl. The presence of these chemical moieties endows these nanomaterials with good solubility in water and outstanding luminescent properties [36]. Fig. 5.b shows the Raman spectra of the samples. For MNP@C-G, two particular broad bands are observed, one at 1514–1657 cm−1 (G-band) and another at 1360–1473 cm−1 (D-band). For this sample, the characteristic aromatic carbon signal is not observed. In sample MNP@C-P a band at 1575-1637 cm−1 can be related to sp2 hybridized carbon network (E2g) (G-band). A signal at 1450–1570 cm−1 can be associated with the presence of aromatic carbon in the structure [38]. Also, a band at 1323–1375 cm−1, known as a disorder band (D-band), can be related to defects in the graphite network (sp3 carbon).

Fig. 6 exhibits the plots of zeta potential as a function of pH for the investigated samples to gain some insights about the colloidal stability and the surface charge behavior of the nanoparticles. The mechanism of charge regulation in the iron oxide nanoparticles is already established in the literature [22,39,40]. The nanoparticle surface behaves as a weak Brönsted diprotic acid, where the surface sites are mostly in the form of FeOH2 + in strong acid medium, FeOH close to the point of zero charge and finally FeO¯ in strongly basic medium, with Fe being the iron present on the particle surface. This mechanism justifies the positive (negative) zeta potential for sample MNP in acidic (alkaline) conditions. Higher absolute values of zeta potential are found for extreme pH conditions, a finding related to the high colloidal stability of the sample in these pH regions. In the case of MNP@C-G, the zeta potential profile also indicates the presence of amino and carboxyl groups on the nanoparticle surface. In acidic pH, the zeta potential reaches a maximum value of +19.8 mV, while in alkaline medium it is -44.7 mV. The more negative zeta potential of this sample in high pH compared to MNP is a robust indication of the presence of a large number of carboxylate groups on the nanocomposite surface. It is also related to the higher colloidal stability of this nanocomposite in this pH region. For sample MNP@C-P, the profile of the pH dependence of the zeta potential indicates a different charge regulation. Indeed, the surface modification of the nanoparticles due to the carbon coating introduces amino groups, which can be protonated to generate a positive charge in a wide range of pH. In acidic medium, the highest value of zeta potential is observed at pH = 2 (+25.5 mV), which may be correlated to the presence of the maximum number of protonated amino groups. With increasing pH, the carboxyl groups are progressively deprotonated leading to carboxylate groups, which generate a net negative charge [18]. Then, the zeta potential reaches a maximum value at pH = 12 (-45,5 mV), where the carboxyl groups are completely deprotonated.Fe

The UV-vis absorption spectra and the photoluminescence (PL) of the MNP@C-G and MNP@C-P samples are shown in Fig. 7 . Typical decomposition of the absorption spectra of the samples are available in Fig. S3. The sample MNP@C-G absorbs in the UV region at 272 nm and 330 nm, which may be attributed to the π-π* transitions of sp2 aromatic domains of the C=C bonds and n→π* transitions at the edge of the carbon lattice, respectively. Besides, absorptions are also observed at 406, 538, and 690 nm, which indicates that this sample also has different surface states. The sample MNP@C-P absorbs in the UV region at 248 nm and shows a shoulder at 305 nm, characteristic of π-π* transitions of sp2 aromatic domains [41]. These bands may be assigned to the C=C and C=N bond transitions, respectively, which generally do not give rise to fluorescence [41,42]. There is also an absorption at 374 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transitions of the C = O band. In the lower energy region, distinct absorption bands at 409, 475, 584, and 684 nm can be seen, indicating that this sample has different surface states [20]. An excitation-dependent PL behavior is observed in carbon-coated samples (see Fig. 7), which is common for pure carbon dots [43]. In sample MNP@C-P, the emission spectra from 410 nm to 875 nm show a change in the photoluminescence intensity as the excitation wavelength varies from 360 nm to 600 nm. Sample MNP@C-G also shows excitation-dependent PL. For this nanocomposite, the emission spectra from 448 nm to 818 nm show a change in luminescence intensity as the excitation lengths range from 360 nm to 600 nm. The maximum wavelength from the decomposition of the emission peaks of samples MNP@C-G and MNP@C-P as a function of the excitation wavelengths is available in Table S1 and Table S2, respectively. For comparison, the absorption spectrum of sample MNP is shown in Fig. S4, and the absorption and luminescence spectra of pure carbon dots prepared from p-phenylenediamine, and glucose are shown in Fig. S5. These results indicate that the behavior of optical and multicolored emission in nanocomposites arises from the carbon-coatings, which have significant similarities with pure carbon dots obtained from the same precursors. Moreover, these results indicate that the distinct organic layers on the surface of the nanocomposites are responsible for the different photoluminescent emission profiles of carbon-coated nanocomposites, since they provide different surface states, leading to multicolored and complex PL behavior. In a previous work, investigating the pH-dependent surface properties of pure Cdots obtained from different precursors, Silva et al. [18] showed that surface hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are associated with blue emission, while amino or hydrazine groups shift emission from blue to green, and the presence of nitrogen in a structure can promote a redshift in the emission.

We successfully synthesized carbon-coated magneto-luminescent nanocomposites based on a core@shell strategy – a nanosized γ-Fe2O3 magnetic core, obtained by coprecipitation, coated with a carbon shell, produced by hydrothermal treatment using different precursors (glucose and p-phenylenediamine). We showed that the conditions of synthesis play an important role in the structural, magnetic, surface, and optical properties of the final nanocomposites. The hydrothermal treatment with D-glucose promoted an increase in the saturation magnetization, which may be associated with a partial conversion of γ-Fe2O3 to Fe3O4 in the nanocomposite core. In the case of the sample synthesized with p-phenylenediamine, a sharp decrease was observed in the saturation magnetization, related to a partial conversion of the maghemite core into hematite. In addition, it was found that the luminescent behavior of nanocomposites with multicolored emissions on long-wavelength originates from the superficial carbon layer combined with the different energy states of the chemical groups attached to the surface, i.e. amino, carbonyl, carboxylate, and hydroxyl groups, which are also responsible for charge regulation and high colloidal stability in aqueous solution. This whole set of results demonstrate that the properties of nanocomposites can be tuned from the precursor reagents to deliver some desired applications.

Atailson Oliveira da Silva: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Alex Fabiano Cortez Campos: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Marcelo Oliveira Rodrigues: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Marcelo Henrique Sousa: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the following Brazilian Agencies: Conselho Nacional de Desenvolvimento Científico e Tecnológico (CNPq), Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior (CAPES) and Fundação de Apoio à Pesquisa do Distrito Federal (FAPDF). M.H.S. acknowledges CNPq grant number 305481/2018-5 and FAPDF grant number 193-00000188/2019-58. The authors would like to thank Dr. Tatiane Oliveira dos Santos from the High-Resolution Microscopy Multi-User Laboratory at the Federal University of Goiás, Brazil, for the electron microscopy measurements.
